mnemonics.title: Step Size Formula
mnemonics.content: α = (rᵀr) / (rᵀAr) - Remember: 'Residual Resides in Numerator, A Rules Denominator'

mnemonics.title: Search Direction
mnemonics.content: d_k = -∇f = r = b - Ax - Think: 'Negative Gradient IS Residual' (NGR = IR)

mnemonics.title: Iteration Formula
mnemonics.content: x_{k+1} = x_k + α_k r_k - Think: 'X New = X Old Plus Alpha Times Residual' (XNOXAR)

acronyms.usage: Matrix type required for guaranteed convergence in Steepest Descent
Faster alternative method for solving SPD linear systems
Another iterative method but not as efficient as SD for general problems

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a ball rolling down an elongated bowl - it bounces side to side (zigzag) taking many oscillations to reach bottom. This is why high condition number causes slow convergence.
Draw concentric ellipses (for quadratic). The gradient points perpendicular to ellipses. Steepest descent goes straight 'across' (perpendicular), hitting another ellipse, creating the zigzag.
Think of climbing down a mountain in the steepest direction - you want to go just far enough to reach the lowest point along that path, not too far (overshoot) or too little (slow progress).

analogies.analogy: Like a hiker lost in fog descending a mountain by always walking in the direction of steepest slope. Each step is optimal locally but may not be the fastest route to the bottom (like zigzag path).
Think of residual as your 'error' or 'distance' from the destination - like how far off you are from the correct answer in a calculation.
High condition number is like a very elongated valley - the hiker takes tiny steps sideways making slow progress. Low condition number is like a circular bowl - quick descent to bottom.
